one_line_takeaway: Casting optimal (epsilon, delta)-DP noise design as an infinite-dimensional DRO problem yields cutting-plane-solvable primal/dual LPs that certify near-optimal, sometimes non-monotone, noise distributions beating analytic Gaussian and truncated Laplace across all privacy budgets, not just asymptotically.
key_finding: The optimization-based mechanisms provably and empirically beat the prior best-in-class mechanisms across privacy regimes, with certified sub-1% optimality gaps computed in seconds. On the motivating Kaggle salary query at (1, 0.2)-DP, the added-noise standard deviation drops from 300.96 INR (analytic Gaussian) and 273.48 INR (truncated Laplace) to 257.68 INR for their method. On synthetic instances the previously-best benchmark (truncated Laplace upper bound plus the Geng et al. lower bound) is shown to be substantially suboptimal, with optimality gaps rising with epsilon and delta (up to ~67% in the reported l1 table, and exceeding 700% under an alternative denominator for (epsilon, delta) = (5, 0.75)); the truncated Laplace mechanism is confirmed asymptotically optimal only in the high-privacy regime and is dominated by the data-dependent optimizer there. A notable structural finding is that optimal noise distributions can be NON-monotone, contradicting a monotonicity assumption implicitly baked into essentially all closed-form benchmark mechanisms. On UCI ML benchmarks the OPT mechanism consistently yields the lowest error among data-independent DP mechanisms, with statistically significant improvements (t-test p < 1e-7 on most datasets).
core_contribution: The paper reframes the design of optimal (epsilon, delta)-differentially private additive-noise mechanisms as an infinite-dimensional distributionally robust optimization (DRO) problem: minimize an expected loss over the space of noise distributions subject to the DP constraints. Its OWN contributions are threefold: (i) it formulates both the data-independent noise problem (P) and the data-dependent noise problem (P') as such DRO problems that admit generic loss functions (including asymmetric ones like the pinball loss) and support constraints; (ii) it proves a strong dual holds and builds converging hierarchies of finite-dimensional linear programs that sandwich the true optimum from above (primal/implementable perturbation) and below (dual/certificate), solvable in seconds via a tailored cutting-plane method; and (iii) unlike prior work, its optimality guarantees are non-asymptotic and unconditional, holding for ANY choice of epsilon and delta rather than only asymptotically as epsilon, delta -> 0 or only within a pre-fixed mechanism family (Gaussian, Laplace, staircase, etc.). Crucially, the specific mechanisms it competes against (analytic Gaussian, truncated Laplace, staircase, uniform-Bernoulli) are CITED prior work reviewed in the introduction, not the paper's own construction; the paper's contribution is the optimization framework that provably matches or beats all of them.
threat_model: This is a genuine privacy/security paper (arXiv cs.CR). The threat model is the standard differential-privacy adversary: an attacker who knows all but one row of a database D, knows the full implementation of the randomized mechanism A (including its parameters), and observes a single published query output omega ~ A(D), then attempts to infer the unknown row (a reconstruction / membership-style inference attack). (epsilon, delta)-DP guarantees that for neighboring databases the output probability ratio is bounded by exp(epsilon) with delta slack, so the adversary cannot confidently estimate the missing row. The paper motivates this concretely (Example 1) with a maximum-likelihood reconstruction attack on the average-salary query over a Kaggle dataset, showing an adversary knowing 193 of 194 PhD-researcher salaries could otherwise back out the 194th. The paper's angle is not a new attack or defense primitive but OPTIMALITY of the defense: given a fixed privacy budget, produce the noise distribution with maximal utility (minimal expected loss).
limitations: Restricted to scalar (one-dimensional) query functions; multi-dimensional queries are only conjectured as future work. Count queries (n_{y=c}, categorical co-occurrence counts) violate the surjectivity Assumption 2/3, so the method produces feasible but potentially overly conservative noise for them. Data-dependent optimization is expensive: while data-independent instances solve within 2.2s, reducing the data-dependent primal-dual gap to 5% took up to 980.44s. The data-dependent formulation requires the query-output weighting w to be either public or itself privatized to avoid leaking information. The mechanisms are numerical/tabular with no closed-form characterization (as the authors note, the optimal Kaggle noise distribution 'does not appear to admit a simple analytical characterization'), and correctness depends on known, bounded query sensitivity and known feature bounds. Convergence requires simultaneously growing support and shrinking granularity; keeping support fixed does not converge.

datasets_used: Kaggle salary dataset (6,704 individuals; 194 PhD researchers) - illustrative motivating example
Synthetic data-independent and data-dependent DP instances (controlled Delta_f, epsilon, delta)
13 UCI classification datasets for DP naive Bayes: post-operative, adult, breast-cancer, contraceptive, dermatology, cylinder-bands, annealing, spect, bank, abalone, spambase, ecoli, absent
14 UCI datasets for DP logistic regression (same 13 plus colon-cancer from LIBSVM, d=2000)
quantitative_results: Kaggle query at (1,0.2)-DP noise std dev: analytic Gaussian 300.96 INR, truncated Laplace 273.48 INR, their method 257.68 INR
Prior (1,0)-DP Laplace mechanism adds std dev 510.28 INR; (1,0.2)-DP Gaussian 689.21 INR on the same example
Certified relative optimality gaps kept strictly < 1% (median 0.28%) across the 100 synthetic (epsilon, delta) combinations
Benchmark suboptimality on synthetic l1 instances up to ~67% (e.g. 67.44% at epsilon=0.02, delta=0.2), growing with epsilon and delta; exceeds 700% for (5,0.75) under the O denominator
Runtime: data-independent problems solved within 2.2 s; data-dependent up to 5.82 s to reach a 10% gap and up to 980.44 s to reach 5%
DP naive Bayes at (1,0.1)-DP on adult: out-of-sample error 20.56% (OPT) vs 21.80% (truncated Laplace) vs 38.82% (Gaussian) vs 17.45% non-private
DP logistic regression at (1,0.1)-DP on dermatology: out-of-sample error 17.85% (OPT) vs 17.93% (TLN) vs 21.38% (GN) vs 16.95% non-private PCD
Statistical significance of OPT over next-best: t-test p < 1e-7 on most UCI datasets (naive Bayes exception dermatology p~1e-1)
baselines_compared: Truncated Laplace mechanism (Geng et al. 2020) - the prior strongest optimality guarantee for generic (epsilon,delta) queries
Near-optimal lower bound of Geng and Viswanath (2015, Thm 8) / Geng et al. (2020)
Classical non-private naive Bayes (NB) and non-private proximal coordinate descent (PCD) as utility references
